---
title: "MalRFinalCode"
output: html_document
date: "2025-12-05"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

```{r}
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Setup and Data Loading
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(zoo)     # Required for calculating rolling averages (form)
library(ranger)  # Fast implementation of Random Forest
library(ggplot2) # For visualization

# Load raw datasets from local directory
games <- read_csv("games.csv")
details <- read_csv("games_details.csv")

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Data Cleaning & Pre-processing
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Filter games to the "Modern 3-Point Era" (2015-Present) to ensure
# the data reflects current style of play.
games_clean <- games %>%
  select(GAME_ID, GAME_DATE_EST, SEASON, HOME_TEAM_ID, VISITOR_TEAM_ID) %>%
  mutate(GAME_DATE_EST = as.Date(GAME_DATE_EST)) %>%
  filter(SEASON >= 2015)

# Merge player details with game context (dates, teams)
master_df <- details %>%
  inner_join(games_clean, by = "GAME_ID")

# Create Target Variable and basic conversions
master_df <- master_df %>%
  mutate(
    # Target Variable: Binary Classification (1 = Made 3+ 3PM, 0 = Under)
    # We choose 2.5 as the standard line for decent 3-point shooters.
    HIT_OVER = ifelse(FG3M >= 3, 1, 0),
    
    # Identify Opponent ID based on whether the player is Home or Away
    OPPONENT_TEAM_ID = ifelse(TEAM_ID == HOME_TEAM_ID, VISITOR_TEAM_ID, HOME_TEAM_ID),
    
    # Convert 'MIN' from "MM:SS" string format to numeric minutes for calculation
    MIN_NUM = sapply(strsplit(as.character(MIN), ":"), function(x) {
      as.numeric(x[1]) + as.numeric(x[2])/60
    })
  ) %>%
  # Remove rows where players didn't play (DNP) to avoid skewing averages with 0s
  filter(!is.na(MIN_NUM)) %>%
  # Sort is critical for accurate rolling averages in the next step
  arrange(PLAYER_ID, GAME_DATE_EST)

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Feature Engineering
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Calculate player-specific rolling metrics to capture "Current Form"
# CRITICAL: We use lag() to ensure we only use data from PREVIOUS games.
# Using current game stats to predict the current game would be data leakage.
master_df <- master_df %>%
  group_by(PLAYER_ID) %>%
  mutate(
    # --- Recent Form (Last 5 Games) ---
    # Captures if a player is "Hot" or "Cold"
    AVG_3PM_LAST_5 = rollmean(lag(FG3M, n=1), k = 5, fill = NA, align = "right"),
    AVG_3PA_LAST_5 = rollmean(lag(FG3A, n=1), k = 5, fill = NA, align = "right"),
    AVG_MIN_LAST_5 = rollmean(lag(MIN_NUM, n=1), k = 5, fill = NA, align = "right"),
    
    # --- Efficiency Trend ---
    # Calculates shooting percentage over the last 5 games
    # We use ifelse to handle division by zero for players with 0 attempts
    PCT_3P_LAST_5 = ifelse(AVG_3PA_LAST_5 > 0, AVG_3PM_LAST_5 / AVG_3PA_LAST_5, 0),
    
    # --- Fatigue Metric ---
    # Days of rest since the previous match
    DAYS_SINCE_LAST_GAME = as.numeric(GAME_DATE_EST - lag(GAME_DATE_EST, n=1)),
    
    # --- Context ---
    # 1 if Home Game, 0 if Away Game
    IS_HOME_GAME = ifelse(TEAM_ID == HOME_TEAM_ID, 1, 0)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# Remove the first 5 rows for each player (NA values generated by rolling window)
# Machine Learning models cannot handle NAs in predictor variables.
model_data <- master_df %>%
  filter(!is.na(AVG_3PM_LAST_5)) %>%
  filter(!is.na(DAYS_SINCE_LAST_GAME))

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 4. Advanced Feature: Opponent Defensive Strength
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step A: Aggregate team-level 3PM per game to see how many 3s were scored total
team_box_scores <- master_df %>%
  group_by(GAME_ID, TEAM_ID, GAME_DATE_EST) %>%
  summarize(TEAM_3PM = sum(FG3M, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Step B: Self-join to determine what the *Opponent* allowed
# If Team A played Team B, Team B's score is Team A's "Points Allowed"
defense_stats <- team_box_scores %>%
  inner_join(team_box_scores, by = "GAME_ID", suffix = c("_OFFENSE", "_DEFENSE")) %>%
  filter(TEAM_ID_OFFENSE != TEAM_ID_DEFENSE) %>%
  rename(
    TEAM_ID = TEAM_ID_OFFENSE,            
    OPPONENT_ID = TEAM_ID_DEFENSE,        
    FG3_ALLOWED = TEAM_3PM_DEFENSE        
  ) %>%
  select(GAME_ID, GAME_DATE_EST_OFFENSE, TEAM_ID, FG3_ALLOWED) %>%
  rename(GAME_DATE_EST = GAME_DATE_EST_OFFENSE)

# Step C: Calculate rolling average of 3s allowed (Defensive Rating proxy)
# This quantifies how "weak" a team's perimeter defense has been recently.
defense_features <- defense_stats %>%
  arrange(TEAM_ID, GAME_DATE_EST) %>%
  group_by(TEAM_ID) %>%
  mutate(
    OPP_AVG_3PM_ALLOWED_L10 = rollmean(lag(FG3_ALLOWED, n=1), k = 10, fill = NA, align = "right")
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(GAME_ID, TEAM_ID, OPP_AVG_3PM_ALLOWED_L10)

# Step D: Merge defensive features back to player data
# We join based on the player's *Opponent* ID to attach that team's defensive stats.
final_model_data <- model_data %>%
  left_join(defense_features, 
            by = c("GAME_ID" = "GAME_ID", "OPPONENT_TEAM_ID" = "TEAM_ID")) %>%
  filter(!is.na(OPP_AVG_3PM_ALLOWED_L10))

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 5. Model Training & Evaluation
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Temporal Train/Test Split
# We train on historical data (2015-2021) and test on the "future" (2022)
# to simulate real-world betting conditions.
train_data <- final_model_data %>% filter(year(GAME_DATE_EST) < 2022)
test_data  <- final_model_data %>% filter(year(GAME_DATE_EST) == 2022)

cat("Training Rows:", nrow(train_data), "\n")
cat("Testing Rows:", nrow(test_data), "\n")

# --- Model 1: Logistic Regression (Baseline) ---
# We use family="binomial" because the target (HIT_OVER) is binary (0 or 1)
baseline_model <- glm(HIT_OVER ~ AVG_3PM_LAST_5 + PCT_3P_LAST_5 + 
                        DAYS_SINCE_LAST_GAME + IS_HOME_GAME + OPP_AVG_3PM_ALLOWED_L10, 
                      data = train_data, 
                      family = "binomial")

# Generate probabilities and apply a 0.5 threshold for classification
test_data$prob_over <- predict(baseline_model, newdata = test_data, type = "response")
test_data$prediction <- ifelse(test_data$prob_over > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Evaluation
conf_matrix <- table(Predicted = test_data$prediction, Actual = test_data$HIT_OVER)
accuracy_log <- sum(diag(conf_matrix)) / sum(conf_matrix)
cat("Logistic Regression Accuracy:", round(accuracy_log, 4), "\n")

# --- Model 2: Random Forest (Ranger) ---
# Random Forest is used to capture non-linear relationships between variables.
cat("Training Random Forest Model...\n")
rf_model <- ranger(
  formula = HIT_OVER ~ AVG_3PM_LAST_5 + PCT_3P_LAST_5 + 
            DAYS_SINCE_LAST_GAME + IS_HOME_GAME + OPP_AVG_3PM_ALLOWED_L10, 
  data = train_data, 
  num.trees = 100,          # Set to 100 for computational efficiency
  importance = "impurity",  # Required to extract Feature Importance later
  classification = TRUE
)

# Predictions
rf_pred <- predict(rf_model, data = test_data)
test_data$rf_prediction <- rf_pred$predictions

# Evaluation
rf_conf_matrix <- table(Predicted = test_data$rf_prediction, Actual = test_data$HIT_OVER)
rf_accuracy <- sum(diag(rf_conf_matrix)) / sum(rf_conf_matrix)
cat("Random Forest Accuracy:", round(rf_accuracy, 4), "\n")

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 6. Visualizations & Insights
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# --- A. Variable Importance Plot ---
# Visualizing which features contributed most to the model's decisions
imp_data <- data.frame(
  Feature = names(importance(rf_model)),
  Importance = importance(rf_model)
)

ggplot(imp_data, aes(x = reorder(Feature, Importance), y = Importance)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Feature Importance: What drives the prediction?",
       subtitle = "Random Forest Impurity Scores",
       x = "Feature", y = "Importance") +
  theme_minimal()

# --- B. Player Predictability Leaderboard (Diverging Lollipop) ---
# Creating a risk assessment tool to identify "Safe" vs "Risky" players
player_performance <- test_data %>%
  mutate(correct = ifelse(prediction == HIT_OVER, 1, 0)) %>%
  group_by(PLAYER_NAME) %>%
  summarize(
    games_played = n(),
    accuracy = mean(correct),
    predicted_overs = sum(prediction),
    actual_overs = sum(HIT_OVER)
  ) %>%
  filter(games_played >= 20) %>% # Filter for sample size significance
  arrange(desc(accuracy))

# Prepare Top 10 / Bottom 10 for plotting
top_10 <- head(player_performance, 10) %>% mutate(Type = "Most Predictable (Safe Bets)")
bottom_10 <- tail(player_performance, 10) %>% mutate(Type = "Least Predictable (Risky)")
plot_data <- rbind(top_10, bottom_10)
avg_acc <- mean(player_performance$accuracy)

# Plotting the Diverging Lollipop Chart
ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = reorder(PLAYER_NAME, accuracy), y = accuracy, color = Type)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = reorder(PLAYER_NAME, accuracy), xend = reorder(PLAYER_NAME, accuracy), 
                   y = avg_acc, yend = accuracy), size = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 5) +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = avg_acc, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  annotate("text", x = 10, y = avg_acc + 0.02, label = "Model Avg", color = "gray50") +
  labs(title = "Predictability Leaderboard",
       subtitle = "Risk Assessment: Safe Bets vs. Volatile Players",
       x = "", y = "Model Accuracy", color = "Player Type") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Least Predictable (Risky)" = "#E74C3C", 
                                "Most Predictable (Safe Bets)" = "#27AE60")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

# --- C. Target Matchups (Opponent Analysis) ---
# Identifying which Opponents are easiest to predict against
# Create Mapping Table to get Team Names
team_map <- details %>% 
  select(TEAM_ID, TEAM_ABBREVIATION) %>% 
  distinct() %>%
  group_by(TEAM_ID) %>% slice(1) %>% ungroup() %>%
  rename(OPP_ABBREVIATION = TEAM_ABBREVIATION)

# Aggregate Accuracy by Opponent
opponent_performance <- test_data %>%
  left_join(team_map, by = c("OPPONENT_TEAM_ID" = "TEAM_ID")) %>%
  group_by(OPP_ABBREVIATION) %>%
  summarize(
    games_played_against = n(),
    accuracy = mean(prediction == HIT_OVER)
  ) %>%
  filter(games_played_against > 50) %>%
  arrange(desc(accuracy))

# Plot Top 10 Target Teams
top_opponents <- head(opponent_performance, 10)

ggplot(top_opponents, aes(x = reorder(OPP_ABBREVIATION, accuracy), y = accuracy, fill = accuracy)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  coord_flip(ylim = c(0.7, 1.0)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#56B4E9", high = "#009E73") +
  labs(title = "Target List: Best Defensive Matchups",
       subtitle = "Model Accuracy by Opponent Team (2022)",
       x = "Opponent", y = "Prediction Accuracy") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```
